scene_title: Rome. Before the Capitol; the Senate sitting above

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: To the Soothsayer. The ides of March are come.

lines.speaker: SOOTHSAYER
lines.text: Ay, Caesar; but not gone.

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: Hail, Caesar! read this schedule.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Trebonius doth desire you to o’er-read,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: At your best leisure, this his humble suit.

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: O Caesar, read mine first; for mine’s a suit

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: That touches Caesar nearer: read it, great Caesar.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: What touches us ourself shall be last served.

lines.speaker: ARTEMIDORUS
lines.text: Delay not, Caesar; read it instantly.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: What, is the fellow mad?

lines.speaker: PUBLIUS
lines.text: Sirrah, give place.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: What, urge you your petitions in the street?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Come to the Capitol.

lines.speaker: POPILIUS
lines.text: I wish your enterprise to-day may thrive.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: What enterprise, Popilius?

lines.speaker: POPILIUS
lines.text: Fare you well.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What said Popilius Lena?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: He wish’d to-day our enterprise might thrive.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I fear our purpose is discovered.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Look, how he makes to Caesar: mark him.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Casca, be sudden, for we fear prevention.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Brutus, what shall be done? If this be known,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Cassius or Caesar never shall turn back,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: For I will slay myself.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Cassius, be constant:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Popilius Lena speaks not of our purposes;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: For, look, he smiles, and Caesar doth not change.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Trebonius knows his time; for, look you, Brutus,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: He draws Mark Antony out of the way.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Where is Metellus Cimber? Let him go,

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: And presently prefer his suit to Caesar.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He is address’d: press near and second him.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Casca, you are the first that rears your hand.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Are we all ready? What is now amiss

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: That Caesar and his senate must redress?

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: Most high, most mighty, and most puissant Caesar,

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: Metellus Cimber throws before thy seat

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: An humble heart—

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I must prevent thee, Cimber.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: These couchings and these lowly courtesies

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Might fire the blood of ordinary men,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And turn pre-ordinance and first decree

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Into the law of children. Be not fond,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: To think that Caesar bears such rebel blood

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: That will be thaw’d from the true quality

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: With that which melteth fools; I mean, sweet words,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Low-crooked court’sies and base spaniel-fawning.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Thy brother by decree is banished:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: If thou dost bend and pray and fawn for him,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I spurn thee like a cur out of my way.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Know, Caesar doth not wrong, nor without cause

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Will he be satisfied.

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: Is there no voice more worthy than my own,

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: To sound more sweetly in great Caesar’s ear

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: For the repealing of my banish’d brother?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I kiss thy hand, but not in flattery, Caesar;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Desiring thee that Publius Cimber may

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Have an immediate freedom of repeal.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: What, Brutus!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Pardon, Caesar; Caesar, pardon:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: As low as to thy foot doth Cassius fall,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: To beg enfranchisement for Publius Cimber.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: I could be well moved, if I were as you;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: If I could pray to move, prayers would move me:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: But I am constant as the northern star,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Of whose true-fix’d and resting quality

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: There is no fellow in the firmament.

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: The skies are painted with unnumber’d sparks,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: They are all fire and everyone doth shine,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: But there’s but one in all doth hold his place:

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: So in the world; ’tis furnish’d well with men,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And men are flesh and blood, and apprehensive;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Yet in the number I do know but one

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: That unassailable holds on his rank,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Unshaked of motion: and that I am he,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Let me a little show it, even in this;

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: That I was constant Cimber should be banish’d,

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: And constant do remain to keep him so.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: O Caesar—

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Hence! wilt thou lift up Olympus?

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: Great Caesar—

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Doth not Brutus bootless kneel?

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Speak, hands for me!

lines.speaker: CAESAR
lines.text: Et tu, Brute! Then fall, Caesar.

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Liberty! Freedom! Tyranny is dead!

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Run hence, proclaim, cry it about the streets.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Some to the common pulpits, and cry out

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: “Liberty, freedom, and enfranchisement!”

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: People and senators, be not affrighted;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Fly not; stand still: ambition’s debt is paid.

lines.speaker: CASCA
lines.text: Go to the pulpit, Brutus.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: And Cassius too.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Where’s Publius?

lines.speaker: CINNA
lines.text: Here, quite confounded with this mutiny.

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: Stand fast together, lest some friend of Caesar’s

lines.speaker: METELLUS
lines.text: Should chance—

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Talk not of standing. Publius, good cheer;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: There is no harm intended to your person,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Nor to no Roman else: so tell them, Publius.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: And leave us, Publius; lest that the people,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Rushing on us, should do your age some mischief.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Do so: and let no man abide this deed,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But we the doers.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Where is Antony?

lines.speaker: TREBONIUS
lines.text: Fled to his house amazed:

lines.speaker: TREBONIUS
lines.text: Men, wives and children stare, cry out and run

lines.speaker: TREBONIUS
lines.text: As it were doomsday.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Fates, we will know your pleasures:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That we shall die, we know; ’tis but the time

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And drawing days out, that men stand upon.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Why, he that cuts off twenty years of life

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Cuts off so many years of fearing death.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Grant that, and then is death a benefit:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: So are we Caesar’s friends, that have abridged

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: His time of fearing death. Stoop, Romans, stoop,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And let us bathe our hands in Caesar’s blood

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Up to the elbows, and besmear our swords:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Then walk we forth, even to the market-place,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And, waving our red weapons o’er our heads,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Let’s all cry “Peace, freedom and liberty!”

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Stoop, then, and wash. How many ages hence

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Shall this our lofty scene be acted over

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: In states unborn and accents yet unknown!

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: How many times shall Caesar bleed in sport,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That now on Pompey’s basis lies along

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: No worthier than the dust!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: So oft as that shall be,

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: So often shall the knot of us be call’d

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: The men that gave their country liberty.

lines.speaker: DECIUS
lines.text: What, shall we forth?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Ay, every man away:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Brutus shall lead; and we will grace his heels

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: With the most boldest and best hearts of Rome.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Soft! who comes here? A friend of Antony’s.

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Thus, Brutus, did my master bid me kneel;

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Thus did Mark Antony bid me fall down;

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: And, being prostrate, thus he bade me say:

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Brutus is noble, wise, valiant, and honest;

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Caesar was mighty, bold, royal, and loving:

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Say I love Brutus, and I honour him;

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Say I fear’d Caesar, honour’d him and loved him.

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: If Brutus will vouchsafe that Antony

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: May safely come to him, and be resolved

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: How Caesar hath deserved to lie in death,

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Mark Antony shall not love Caesar dead

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: So well as Brutus living; but will follow

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: The fortunes and affairs of noble Brutus

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: Thorough the hazards of this untrod state

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: With all true faith. So says my master Antony.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Thy master is a wise and valiant Roman;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I never thought him worse.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Tell him, so please him come unto this place,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He shall be satisfied; and, by my honour,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Depart untouch’d.

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: I’ll fetch him presently.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I know that we shall have him well to friend.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I wish we may: but yet have I a mind

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That fears him much; and my misgiving still

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Falls shrewdly to the purpose.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But here comes Antony.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Welcome, Mark Antony.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: O mighty Caesar! dost thou lie so low?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Are all thy conquests, glories, triumphs, spoils,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Shrunk to this little measure? Fare thee well.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I know not, gentlemen, what you intend,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Who else must be let blood, who else is rank:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: If I myself, there is no hour so fit

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: As Caesar’s death hour, nor no instrument

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Of half that worth as those your swords, made rich

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: With the most noble blood of all this world.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I do beseech ye, if you bear me hard,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Now, whilst your purpled hands do reek and smoke,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Fulfil your pleasure. Live a thousand years,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I shall not find myself so apt to die:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: No place will please me so, no mean of death,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: As here by Caesar, and by you cut off,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: The choice and master spirits of this age.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: O Antony, beg not your death of us.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Though now we must appear bloody and cruel,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: As, by our hands and this our present act,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: You see we do, yet see you but our hands

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And this the bleeding business they have done:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Our hearts you see not; they are pitiful;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And pity to the general wrong of Rome—

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: As fire drives out fire, so pity pity—

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Hath done this deed on Caesar. For your part,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: To you our swords have leaden points, Mark Antony:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Our arms, in strength of malice, and our hearts

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Of brothers’ temper, do receive you in

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: With all kind love, good thoughts, and reverence.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Your voice shall be as strong as any man’s

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: In the disposing of new dignities.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Only be patient till we have appeased

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: The multitude, beside themselves with fear,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And then we will deliver you the cause,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Why I, that did love Caesar when I struck him,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Have thus proceeded.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I doubt not of your wisdom.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Let each man render me his bloody hand:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: First, Marcus Brutus, will I shake with you;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Next, Caius Cassius, do I take your hand;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Now, Decius Brutus, yours; now yours, Metellus;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Yours, Cinna; and, my valiant Casca, yours;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Though last, not least in love, yours, good Trebonius.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Gentlemen all—alas, what shall I say?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: My credit now stands on such slippery ground,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That one of two bad ways you must conceit me,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Either a coward or a flatterer.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That I did love thee, Caesar, O, ’tis true:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: If then thy spirit look upon us now,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Shall it not grieve thee dearer than thy death,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To see thy Anthony making his peace,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Shaking the bloody fingers of thy foes,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Most noble! in the presence of thy corse?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Had I as many eyes as thou hast wounds,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Weeping as fast as they stream forth thy blood,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: It would become me better than to close

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: In terms of friendship with thine enemies.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Pardon me, Julius! Here wast thou bay’d, brave hart;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Here didst thou fall; and here thy hunters stand,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Sign’d in thy spoil, and crimson’d in thy lethe.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: O world, thou wast the forest to this hart;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And this, indeed, O world, the heart of thee.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: How like a deer, strucken by many princes,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Dost thou here lie!

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Mark Antony—

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Pardon me, Caius Cassius:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: The enemies of Caesar shall say this;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Then, in a friend, it is cold modesty.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I blame you not for praising Caesar so;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: But what compact mean you to have with us?

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Will you be prick’d in number of our friends;

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Or shall we on, and not depend on you?

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Therefore I took your hands, but was, indeed,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Sway’d from the point, by looking down on Caesar.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Friends am I with you all and love you all,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Upon this hope, that you shall give me reasons

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Why and wherein Caesar was dangerous.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Or else were this a savage spectacle:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Our reasons are so full of good regard

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: That were you, Antony, the son of Caesar,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: You should be satisfied.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That’s all I seek:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And am moreover suitor that I may

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Produce his body to the market-place;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And in the pulpit, as becomes a friend,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Speak in the order of his funeral.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: You shall, Mark Antony.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Brutus, a word with you.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: You know not what you do: do not consent

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: That Antony speak in his funeral:

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: Know you how much the people may be moved

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: By that which he will utter?

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: By your pardon;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: I will myself into the pulpit first,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And show the reason of our Caesar’s death:

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: What Antony shall speak, I will protest

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: He speaks by leave and by permission,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And that we are contented Caesar shall

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Have all true rites and lawful ceremonies.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: It shall advantage more than do us wrong.

lines.speaker: CASSIUS
lines.text: I know not what may fall; I like it not.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Mark Antony, here, take you Caesar’s body.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: You shall not in your funeral speech blame us,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: But speak all good you can devise of Caesar,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: And say you do’t by our permission;

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Else shall you not have any hand at all

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: About his funeral: and you shall speak

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: In the same pulpit whereto I am going,

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: After my speech is ended.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Be it so;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: I do desire no more.

lines.speaker: BRUTUS
lines.text: Prepare the body then, and follow us.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: O, pardon me, thou bleeding piece of earth,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That I am meek and gentle with these butchers!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Thou art the ruins of the noblest man

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That ever lived in the tide of times.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Woe to the hand that shed this costly blood!

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Over thy wounds now do I prophesy—

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Which, like dumb mouths, do ope their ruby lips,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To beg the voice and utterance of my tongue—

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: A curse shall light upon the limbs of men;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Domestic fury and fierce civil strife

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Shall cumber all the parts of Italy;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Blood and destruction shall be so in use

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And dreadful objects so familiar

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That mothers shall but smile when they behold

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Their infants quarter’d with the hands of war;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: All pity choked with custom of fell deeds:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: And Caesar’s spirit, ranging for revenge,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: With Ate by his side come hot from hell,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Shall in these confines with a monarch’s voice

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Cry “Havoc,” and let slip the dogs of war;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: That this foul deed shall smell above the earth

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: With carrion men, groaning for burial.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: You serve Octavius Caesar, do you not?

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: I do, Mark Antony.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Caesar did write for him to come to Rome.

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: He did receive his letters, and is coming;

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: And bid me say to you by word of mouth—

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: O Caesar!—Seeing the body.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Thy heart is big, get thee apart and weep.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Passion, I see, is catching; for mine eyes,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Seeing those beads of sorrow stand in thine,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Began to water. Is thy master coming?

lines.speaker: SERVANT
lines.text: He lies to-night within seven leagues of Rome.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Post back with speed, and tell him what hath chanced:

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Here is a mourning Rome, a dangerous Rome,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: No Rome of safety for Octavius yet;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Hie hence, and tell him so. Yet, stay awhile;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Thou shalt not back till I have borne this corse

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Into the market-place: there shall I try,

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: In my oration, how the people take

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: The cruel issue of these bloody men;

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: According to the which, thou shalt discourse

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: To young Octavius of the state of things.

lines.speaker: ANTONY
lines.text: Lend me your hand.

stage_directions.text: A crowd of people; among them Artemidorus and the Soothsayer. Flourish. Enter Caesar, Brutus, Cassius, Casca, Decius, Metellus, Trebonius, Cinna, Antony, Lepidus, Popilius, Publius, and others.
Caesar goes up to the Senate-House, the rest following.
Casca first, then the other Conspirators and Marcus Brutus stab Caesar.

glosses.definition: Caesar's taunt to the Soothsayer — implying the feared day has arrived without harm; the Soothsayer's reply 'but not gone' completes the prophecy's edge
a written petition; a scroll (Artemidorus's warning letter)
read over; peruse

glosses.phrase: What touches us ourself shall be last served
glosses.definition: Caesar's fatal hubris — putting his own safety last, from a public-service ideology that costs him his life
fellow (condescending address to a social inferior)
undertaking — here euphemistic for the assassination plot
being forestalled; having the plot stopped before executed
steadfast; resolute — the Stoic virtue; repeatedly invoked as Cassius slips into panic
immediately present his petition
raises your hand (first to strike)
powerful; mighty (from French)
bowings; prostrations (physical obeisance)
established law and original decree — the fixed constitutional order
foolish (not modern 'loving')
fawning like a spaniel dog — obsequious flattery
low bows with bent knees; Renaissance court etiquette
kick you aside like a mongrel dog

glosses.phrase: Caesar doth not wrong, nor without cause
glosses.definition: Caesar does no wrong, and not without cause — Jonson famously ridiculed this line as self-contradicting; it has been emended since the 18th century
restoration of citizenship; granting of political rights (for an exile, the undoing of banishment)
steadfast as Polaris — Caesar claims absolute political stability; the boast is immediately refuted by 23 wounds
firmly-fixed and motionless character — the North Star alone does not move across the night sky
equal among the stars
capable of understanding; sentient (not modern 'anxious')
move a whole mountain — Mount Olympus was the home of the gods; proverbial for impossible effort
in vain; without result
Casca's cry as he strikes first — the conspirators' agreed signal; 'hands' stand in for voice

Latin: 'and you, Brutus?' — Caesar's famous dying reproach to Brutus. The phrasing is Shakespeare's invention; Suetonius reports Caesar spoke Greek, 'καὶ σύ, τέκνον' ('and you, child'); Plutarch says he said nothing but pulled his toga over his face. The exact Latin is Shakespeare's compressed, English-inflected formulation
Cinna's slogan for the assassination — the conspirators frame the killing as the Republic reasserting itself against incipient monarchy
public speaking platforms (rostra) in the Forum, from which citizens addressed the crowd
the death that ambition has earned — Brutus's first moralising of the killing
confused; bewildered
the Last Judgment; the apocalypse — an anachronistic Christian image for pagan panic

glosses.phrase: abridged / His time of fearing death
glosses.definition: shortened his time of fearing to die — Brutus's sophistical consolation

glosses.phrase: bathe our hands in Caesar's blood
glosses.definition: wash our hands in Caesar's blood — Brutus's ritual sanctification of murder; an image that Antony will invert in 3.2
this noble scene of ours — Cassius imagines the assassination re-staged in future plays in unknown languages, a meta-theatrical gesture
languages not yet invented — a prophecy fulfilled by Shakespeare's own play
the pedestal of Pompey's statue, at whose base Caesar falls — poetic justice (Plutarch notes this detail)
grant; deign to allow
through
uncertain, untested political condition

glosses.phrase: misgiving still / Falls shrewdly to the purpose
glosses.definition: my forebodings hit the mark — Cassius's accurate instinct, which Brutus overrides
bled — a medical metaphor for killing, dark from Antony's point of view
festering; needing to be bled (of an infected humour)

glosses.phrase: purpled hands do reek and smoke
glosses.definition: bloody hands steam and smoke — the fresh blood still warm
select and chief spirits — the best men; Antony flatters the conspirators

glosses.phrase: pity to the general wrong of Rome
glosses.definition: pity for the general wrong done to Rome — pity for the state overrode personal pity for Caesar
our swords are blunted (not to be used against you)
distributing new offices — Cassius promises Antony a share in the spoils
Cinna the conspirator — to be distinguished from Cinna the Poet of 3.3

glosses.phrase: Though last, not least in love
glosses.definition: proverbial; Antony's ritual handclasp with each conspirator in turn
Antony pretends to fear being thought one or the other — his false humility
corpse
brought to bay, brave stag — the hunting metaphor; pun on hart/heart
the huntsmen stand around their kill (the conspirators positioned around Caesar)
marked with your blood; badged with the kill (a hunting ritual in which hunters dip their fingers in the stag's blood)
stained crimson in your life's blood ('lethe' = life-blood, obsolete usage; not the river Lethe of forgetfulness)
deer struck down by many princes — extending the hunt metaphor; puns again on deer/dear

glosses.phrase: prick'd in number of our friends
glosses.definition: marked down on the list of our friends (a proscription metaphor)
the Rostra, the public speaker's platform in the Forum
the funeral ceremony; the rites

glosses.phrase: I know not what may fall
glosses.definition: I don't know what may happen — Cassius's prescient unease
Caesar's corpse; 'earth' in the biblical sense of mortal clay

glosses.phrase: meek and gentle with these butchers
glosses.definition: mild and civil with these slaughterers — Antony's true view, now spoken alone

glosses.phrase: Domestic fury and fierce civil strife
glosses.definition: internal rage and savage civil war — Antony's prophecy, fulfilled in the historical civil wars of 44–31 BCE
encumber; burden; trouble
cut into four pieces; mutilated
habituation to cruel deeds
Greek goddess of ruin, reckless impulse, and delusion — she brings on divine vengeance
the medieval English battle-cry giving soldiers permission to plunder; proclaiming 'havoc' signalled no-quarter warfare. Shakespeare's military-technical anachronism

glosses.phrase: let slip the dogs of war
glosses.definition: release the war-dogs (unleashed mastiffs); launch total war. The play's most-quoted declaration of civil strife
dead men rotting unburied; corpses
about 21 miles — a league being roughly three miles

glosses.phrase: borne this corse / Into the market-place
glosses.definition: carried Caesar's body to the Forum — the setting for the funeral oration to come
cruel outcome (of the murder)

annotations.title: 'Constant as the northern star' — the boast that precedes the wounds
annotations.body:
Caesar's great self-description — 'But I am constant as the northern star, / Of whose true-fix'd and resting quality / There is no fellow in the firmament' — is the play's most extraordinary set-up for dramatic irony. Between the declaration of cosmic steadfastness and the first stab, seven lines pass. The constancy that Caesar claims for himself is the constancy Cassius has been mocking for four acts ('Ye gods, it doth amaze me / A man of such a feeble temper should / So get the start of the majestic world').

Shakespeare writes the stage-Caesar as a man whose public self-image has diverged fatally from his private body. The seizures, the deafness in his left ear (1.2 L208), the fever in Spain (1.2 L116), the illiteracy of his own will's terms that Antony will reveal (3.2) — all have been built up across the play as rebukes to the northern-star image. The audience does not need historical Caesar; it has been given the public speech and the private body as parallel texts. The assassination is where they collide.

Notice also what the speech is for: it refuses Cimber's plea for his brother's recall. Caesar is being asked for a routine act of political clemency. The grandeur of the refusal — no human circumstance can move the one unmoved mover — turns a minor pardon into an occasion for absolute rule. Shakespeare is careful: the assassination is not unprovoked. It is committed on a man who, at the moment of his death, is claiming exemption from the normal political process of giving and taking.

annotations.title: 'Et tu, Brute': Shakespeare's Latin, not Caesar's
annotations.body:
Caesar's dying reproach is the most recognisable line in the play and among the most widely-misunderstood. The Latin is Shakespeare's compression, not the historical Caesar's last words.

The ancient sources disagree. Suetonius (*Divus Iulius* §82) reports that Caesar said nothing when stabbed, except that some thought he spoke to Brutus in Greek: *καὶ σύ, τέκνον* — 'and you, child'. Plutarch's *Life of Caesar* §66 says Caesar groaned, covered his face with his toga, and said nothing. Dio Cassius reports the same silence. The Greek *καὶ σύ* became the textual tradition, sometimes expanded by later authors to the Latin *et tu* formulation, but Shakespeare's specific phrasing — 'Et tu, Brute! Then fall, Caesar.' — is not in any ancient source.

Shakespeare's Latin is in fact more compressed, more English, and more accusatory than any of his sources. 'Brute' (vocative of Brutus) personalises the reproach where Suetonius's 'child' generalises it; 'then fall, Caesar' makes the assassination a decision rather than a fate. The line has become cultural shorthand for 'betrayal by a close friend,' partly because Shakespeare's version is the one that took.

A modern reader should hear, underneath the famous Latin, the textual history: this is not what Caesar said; it is what Shakespeare made Caesar say.
sources: J. Russell Brown (ed.), New Penguin *Julius Caesar* (1967)
Miles Hollingworth, *Saint Augustine of Hippo* (2013), app. on Caesar's last words

annotations.title: 'Bathe our hands in Caesar's blood': ritual the play will not permit
annotations.body:
Brutus's instruction to the conspirators to dip their hands in Caesar's blood and walk through the market-place displaying bloody swords is the physical gesture that matches the ritual framing of 2.1 ('sacrificers, but not butchers'). Brutus still believes the assassination is a sacred act that, correctly performed, will read to Rome as such.

Shakespeare immediately undercuts the ritual. Cassius's interjection — 'How many ages hence / Shall this our lofty scene be acted over / In states unborn and accents yet unknown!' — is uncanny meta-theatre: the conspirators are already imagining themselves as historical set-pieces, and Shakespeare is (from 1599) writing the exact historical set-piece they imagine. But the meta-theatre cuts against the ritual. By saying 'scene,' Cassius has named what Brutus wishes were a rite. The act is performance, not sacrament.

Antony's entrance a few dozen lines later finishes the job. The bloody hands Brutus means as ritual badges Antony will reframe, in 3.2, as the 'purpled hands' of murderers. The image is the same; the meaning has inverted. Shakespeare is showing that Brutus's ritual depends on a hermeneutic consent that Rome is about to withhold.
sources: David Kastan, 'Proud Majesty Made a Subject', *SQ* 37 (1986)

annotations.title: Antony negotiating: the second mistake to match the first
annotations.body:
Antony's meeting with the conspirators is a masterclass in political negotiation under duress. He shakes each assassin's hand, declares himself friend to all, asks only two concessions: that he be told 'Why and wherein Caesar was dangerous' (L218), and that he be permitted to speak at the funeral (L223–226). Brutus grants both. Cassius privately objects. Brutus overrules.

This is Brutus's second catastrophic decision, exactly mirroring the first. In 2.1 he spared Antony's life; now he grants Antony the funeral-speech platform. Either decision alone is survivable. Together they hand Antony the means of vengeance. Shakespeare is building a pattern: Brutus's principled instincts consistently produce disastrous tactical outcomes. The integrity that recommends him to the conspiracy is the quality that ruins it.

Antony's own tell, in his soliloquy (L250ff), confirms what the conspirators cannot see: the handshake, the friendship-language, the grief — all cover an already-formed intention to unleash 'Domestic fury and fierce civil strife.' Shakespeare's trick is dramatic: the audience hears Antony's soliloquy before the funeral, so the speech of 3.2 arrives as the fulfillment of a promise we have already heard him make.

annotations.title: 'Cry Havoc, and let slip the dogs of war'
annotations.body:
'Havoc' is not a decorative word. In medieval English military law, the cry 'havoc' was the specific command given to soldiers authorising them to plunder and kill without restraint — the giving-up of quarter. By the 14th century, English monarchs (notably Richard II, 1385) had legislated *against* crying havoc without royal permission, precisely because the cry functioned as a legal permission to kill civilians. Shakespeare's audience would have recognised the vocabulary as English martial law, not Roman.

Another English anachronism, then, doing specific work. Antony's 'Cry havoc' is not generic violence; it is the technical command for total war, delivered in the English vocabulary Shakespeare's audience would have recognised from their own statutes. The sword will not be put up, the houses will be burned, the distinction between combatant and civilian will be suspended. The Roman civil wars of 43–31 BCE fulfilled this prophecy: proscriptions, property seizures, mass executions of senators (which Messala will report in 4.3). Antony has been a Roman general until this line; from 'Cry havoc' onwards he is a figure of unrestrained war.

The phrase, like 'Et tu, Brute,' entered the language. It too has largely lost its technical meaning. Modern usage reads 'havoc' as mess or chaos; the original was martial and specific.
sources: M. H. Keen, *The Laws of War in the Late Middle Ages* (1965)

trials.prompt: 'Constant as the northern star' (L59) is ironic because:
options: Caesar is claiming cosmic constancy in the seven lines before being stabbed twenty-three times; the boast is the set-up for its refutation
Stars were not known to Romans
The audience doesn't know the line
Shakespeare's Latin invention; Suetonius reports Caesar said nothing or spoke Greek ('καὶ σύ, τέκνον' — 'and you, child'), Plutarch reports silence

trials.prompt: Brutus's instruction to 'bathe our hands in Caesar's blood' and display bloody swords is an attempt to:
options: Enforce a ritual reading of the assassination as sacrifice, not murder — but Cassius's meta-theatrical 'lofty scene' language and Antony's later 'purpled hands' image will invert the meaning

trials.prompt: Brutus grants Antony both life (2.1) and the funeral-speech platform (3.1). The combined effect is:
options: A pattern in which Brutus's principled instincts produce catastrophic tactical outcomes; together these two decisions hand Antony the means of destroying the conspiracy

trials.prompt: 'Cry havoc, and let slip the dogs of war' uses:
options: Technical medieval-English martial vocabulary — 'havoc' was the specific cry authorising unrestricted plunder and killing, legislated against by English monarchs; another deliberate anachronism in the Roman setting

trials.prompt: After the murder, what does Antony ask the conspirators for?
options: His own safety and a chance to leave Rome
Two things: to be told why Caesar was dangerous, and to speak at the funeral — Brutus grants both over Cassius's objection
Time to bury his friend privately

trials.prompt: Antony's soliloquy over Caesar's body (L250ff) tells the audience:
options: That Antony is truly making peace
That his handshake with the conspirators was theatre; he is already planning to unleash 'Domestic fury and fierce civil strife'
